text: The surgeon performs intercostal blocks using a green needle on a thin patient, inserting the needle from the outside to administer local anesthesia.
The surgeon administers a long line of intercostal blocks using bupivacaine with adrenaline. The Articentio Maryland instrument is introduced to begin taking down visible adhesions.
The surgeon carefully takes down adhesions before placing the second instrument, using bipolar instruments to minimize energy dissipation.
The surgeon inserts a second instrument, a bipolar wristed device, into the third intercostal space. The Maryland instrument is prepared for use, primarily for delivering bipolar energy.

The surgeon begins the thymectomy by meticulously removing adipose tissue from the posterior aspect of the sternum at its lower portion, followed by the removal of fat from the diaphragm, ensuring thorough clearance for a complete thymectomy.
The surgeon uses a versatile instrument capable of rotating ninety degrees left or right to navigate the surgical plane around a small thymoma, demonstrating the technique similar to robotic surgery.
The surgeon carefully removes fat from the diaphragm, exposing the diaphragm muscles, while attempting to lift the right inferior pole of the thymus gland.

The surgeon uses the left-sided instrument to retract tissue while the right-sided instrument identifies the dissection plane. The pericardium is visible, being lifted with one instrument as the Maryland is used to dissect and peel tissue off the pericardium.
The surgeon uses VATS wristed instruments to reach the left inferior pole of the thymus, with plans to employ a sub-xiphoid camera towards the end for enhanced visualization of the opposite side.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to dissect along the pleura, angling it at right angles to reach the opposite side of the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the opposite pleura, revealing the underlying structures. After removing the thymus from the phrenic nerve on the right side, the surgical field transitions to include both lungs.
The surgeon moves back to the right side to detach the thymus from the pericardium, progressing towards the right phrenic nerve for further dissection.
The surgeon visually locates the phrenic nerve, pointing to its position in the surgical field. The nerve is clearly visible despite the patient's less fatty tissue composition.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to dissect near the phrenic nerve, leaving a visible margin around the nerve despite the limited view.

The surgeon works along the superior vena cava (SVC), addressing a small amount of bleeding in the thymus. The blood supply to the thymus is noted to originate from the inferior pole, extending laterally and superiorly.
The surgeon encounters bleeding while working laterally near a small vessel from the reamer vein and artery, prompting a switch to a bipolar instrument.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to control bleeding while following the reamer vein, carefully dissecting and removing tissue from the posterior aspect of the sternum.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to finish off the dissection at the opposite inferior pole of the thymus, reaching deep into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to peel tissue off the pericardium and further remove tissue from the back of the sternum.
The surgeon uses the subxiphoid temoport to obtain a clear view of the left phrenic nerve, addressing the challenge of visualizing the opposite phrenic nerve during thymectomy.

The surgeon observes the phrenic nerve, which is partially obscured by deep fat tissue. A large parasophageal hernia is visible behind the heart, causing upward displacement and reducing space in the anterior mediastinum. The surgeon maintains some fatty tissue adjacent to the phrenic nerve for safety during the thymectomy.

The surgeon uses the lemur artery and vein as landmarks, visible at the top of the screen, while attempting to locate the innominate vein. The lemur vein is seen descending to join the innominate vein, which serves as another landmark. The surgeon navigates through fatty tissue while following these anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon navigates up into the neck, following the lemur vein to the left superior pole of the thymus. The innominate vein is visible as the surgeon approaches the right superior pole, using a ligature hook instrument for dissection.

The surgeon uses an instrument with a hook attachment to perform ligature on the innominate vein and its thymic vein branch, demonstrating the ability to switch between hook and ligature functions with a button click.

The surgeon uses a wristed instrument to retract tissue while employing a ligature to identify the thymic vein. The innominate vein is visible below the ligature as the surgeon pulls down the right superior pole of the thymus. The surgical field appears fatty and confined, impacting the meticulousness of the thymectomy.
The surgeon cuts the right superior pole of the thymus, then carefully dissects the thymic vein from the innominate vein after identifying it.

The surgeon releases tissue and follows the innominate vein across to the lemur vein, carefully dissecting and removing a portion of the thymus from beneath the innominate vein, ensuring safe tissue handling.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to manipulate the left superior pole of the thymus, cutting it slightly short. The dissection occurs between the innominate vein and the lemur vein, with visible tissue manipulation in the neck area.

The surgeon utilizes wristed instruments to perform a thymectomy, employing both a sub-xiphoid camera and a right-sided camera. A surgical bag is introduced through the sub-xiphoid port to collect excised tissue.

The surgeon uses eight-millimeter robotic ports in the right chest and a ten-millimeter sub-xiphoid port to facilitate the removal of the tumor. The final result of the thymectomy is visible, showing the surgical field after tumor extraction.
